SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper supports its main claim with experiments on three datasets (DementiaBank, Cinderella, ABCD) using multiple classifiers and ensembles (Section 4.1, 4.3). However, there are no mathematical derivations and important experimental details are missing or unclear (no explicit train/test splits, validation procedure, or statistical significance testing). The use of Bojanowski et al. embeddings to handle OOV words is reasonable and described (Section 4.2.1), but some methodological choices (e.g., preprocessing decisions, ensemble weighting) are not fully justified, leaving gaps that prevent full technical verification.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The approach adapts known techniques: word adjacency (complex networks) and embedding-based enrichment (citing Perozzi et al. and Bojanowski et al.) to the MCI detection task (Sections 4.2 and related discussion). Applying network enrichment to short clinical transcripts and combining with multi-view/ensemble learning is an application-driven extension rather than a fundamentally new methodological breakthrough. The paper positions itself relative to prior work but the core techniques are incremental adaptations of existing methods.

CLARITY.justification: The paper has a recognizable structure (datasets, feature sets, classification) and lists the network measures used clearly (ten measurements in Section 4.2.1). However, the provided text contains many repetitions, duplicated sentences and formatting/artifact issues that impede reading (repeated paragraphs across sections), and some experimental details and contributions are not stated concisely early on. Notation is minimal but overall presentation requires editing for clarity.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The work evaluates multiple feature types (complex network measures, linguistic metrics, BoW), several classifiers, and ensemble/multi-view combinations across three datasets, which gives breadth to the evaluation (Sections 4.2.3, 4.3). Important analyses are missing: there is no clear ablation isolating the benefit of network enrichment versus plain networks, limited hyperparameter/tuning details (only embedding defaults are listed), no sensitivity analyses, and no discussion of failure modes or limitations, which reduces reproducibility and depth.

IMPACT.justification: Detecting MCI from speech transcripts is an important clinical/NLP problem and the paper demonstrates that complex networks can be useful across English and Portuguese datasets (claims of outperforming linguistic features and multi-view improvements). Nevertheless, the methodological novelty is moderate and results are described as comparable to prior accuracy ranges (60–85%), so the contribution is likely to have moderate, not transformative, impact on the field.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic (automatic MCI detection from transcripts using NLP/ML techniques) fits the scope of top NLP/ML venues and the methods (embeddings, network measures, classifiers, ensembles) are within community expectations (Sections 4.2–4.3). However, the presentation issues and some missing experimental rigor (detailed evaluation protocol, statistical tests) make the submission borderline for a top-tier conference without revision.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares its network features against linguistic metrics (Coh-Metrix/Coh-Metrix-Dementia) and BoW baselines and references prior works (multiple citations throughout), and reports ensemble/multi-view gains. Yet it lacks explicit comparison to the strongest state-of-the-art baselines with matched experimental conditions, does not report variance or statistical significance, and it is unclear whether prior reported results are directly comparable (no explicit same-split or cross-validation details).
